segments.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
If you'd like to be able to listen to the show without ads,
and have full access to bonus content, that's an option.
To find out how, please go to dogmanencounters.com forward slash podcast.
Tonight's guest is Big D.
Big D, thanks so much for coming.
Hey, thanks for having me on, Vic.
Well, thanks for being here.
We appreciate your time.
Please give us a brief bio on yourself.
Well, I'm originally from Mississippi, the northern part of Mississippi.
I grew up here, lived here basically all my life, with the exception of the time I spent in the military.
Outdoorsman, like to hunt, fish, all those good things.
I really like living where I live.
We're in a rural area, and it's really nice.
I like to hunt and fish, like I said, and it's, you know, really conducive for that.
So, you know, that's pretty much it.
I'm married, got a few kids, and just like enjoying life.
Well, as long as you can say that, then it doesn't get any better.
So that's a great thing.
And thank you so much for your service to our country.
I appreciate it.
You're very welcome.
I do.
Big D, you joined the Army in 87 and worked as an MP, but in what capacity?
Well, when I first got to Europe, I was actually assigned to the provost marshal's
office. So usually MPs are assigned to an MP unit. Mine was a little different. I was
actually assigned to the provost marshal's office. And I worked as a traffic accident investigator,
as well as a military police investigator, the latter part of my career.
Sounds like you got into your fair share of interesting things. And with that in mind,
What was the most exciting thing you did wind up getting into while you're working as an
MP?
Well, outside of my experience that I'm going to talk about, you know, one of the
things that that was always fun for me was usually in October, you know, they had a lot
of fest and I played music at the time as well.
So it was a little band.
So we were able to play fests and that was really a lot of fun.
It was a good downtime, you know, to get out on the economy, as they used to say, and mix with the locals.
And so that was a lot of fun for me.
I really enjoyed doing that.
Well, I bet you did.
And while you're working as an MP, from what I understand, you're working night shift when you had your dogman encounter.
Did you normally work the late shift like that or was that just a rare thing?
No, we would mix and match. Whatever the needs of the MP station, the Provost Marshal's
office, whatever the need was, I was filling in at night, basically helping the death sergeant.
And if it was slow, then I would take a ride through the housing areas, like on a patrol,
just to check things out. So by happenstance, I happened to be working that night.
LUCK OF THE DRAW, HUH?
Mm-hmm.
Yeah, I'd say. I guess you could consider it to be that way. It's hard to say, though.

Your encounter happened in Germany What can you tell us about the place where it happened there Well I was stationed in Wiesbaden It about maybe listeners would know it probably about 30 minutes from Frankfurt just across the Rhine River from Mainz in the Rhine River Valley
Really a great place to be stationed as far as being stationed in Germany.
Pretty mild climate, you know, due to the Rhine River, that valley we were in.
I only saw it snow once, so I was pretty fortunate.
Like I said, pretty mild, you know, due to the location of Germany, you know, on the globe.
I really enjoyed the summers, you know, really, really super long days.
You know, people in the northern part of the United States understand what I'm talking about.
But I really enjoyed those long summer days.
It was really nice in the summer.
Sounds like you had a really good assignment there then.
Oh, yeah, it was I was very fortunate to be stationed where I was stationed, you know, with some really good people.
I think most anybody that's ever, you know, served in any capacity would tell you that, you know, you make friends and those friendships are forever.
I've got a buddy that I haven't seen in person for 20 something, no, 30 something years.
And we can get on the phone and it's just like, you know, 1991 again.
And so it was a good experience for me.
Well, that's great.
I'm glad you're still in communication with that person.
And yeah, I'm so glad that that assignment wound up to be such a good one.
That's great.
Now, Big D, a lot of parts of Germany are pretty open.
Other parts are pretty heavily forested.
As far as the part where you were assigned goes, was it open or more in the heavily forested side?
Well, Wiesbaden, the city itself, it was not very open at all. Now, where the air base was stationed, it was associated with the access road that you would travel to get to the air base.
There were fields on either side, so there was a lot of farming that went on there.
you know you wouldn't have to travel very far at all get out on the economy as they say and you
could get into some forested areas and you know I wouldn't say that that particular area was heavily
forested because there wasn't mostly it was agriculture so it was pretty open then if that's
the case yeah yep yeah it must have been a lot of people listening to the show tonight have no idea
what kind of a negative effect reporting a dogman encounter could have for a military career
With that in mind, please talk us through the ramifications of that.
Well, the ramifications were I didn't say anything about it.
I probably didn't mention it for at least two years, maybe right before I left.
And that was with, as a matter of fact, with my close friend that I was mentioning.
I just kept it to myself.
You know, that's not something particularly at that time that I would have been comfortable.
You know, I wouldn't want everybody to know, you know, being an MP and, you know, I'm seeing dogmen.
It probably wouldn't have been very good for my career.
So I kept that under wraps.
Yeah, I can't say I blame you at all.
I can't.
From what I understand, in 81 or 82, you and your stepbrother had an experience one night that might have been cryptid related.
What more can you tell us about that?
And, you know, Vic, this really didn't come to mind until later on in life.
You know, when you think back, it actually started when I kind of got interested more into the cryptids.
And this is long after I'd gotten back home out of the service.
But we were traveling to a pretty rural area, basically going out to a local swimming hole, if you will.
It had a boat ramp and some other things.
And it was late at night.
I don't recall the time.
It was probably midnight or after.
At any rate, we decided that we both needed to use the restroom.
So he pulled over.
and it was, I just remember that night it was pitch black. It was, and you know, we're way out
in the country, so there aren't any street lights there. You know, there's no kind of,
I don't recall a moon at all. Cause it was just, I remember being pitch black.
So he shut the car off and we got out. I got on the passenger side and him on the driver's side.
And as we were standing there taking care of our business, I was facing some, a wooded area
And we heard something, or I heard it, I guess, first, something coming through the woods.
At first I thought, well, maybe it's a deer or maybe it's a cow or maybe it's this or that.
But it continued coming toward us.
And as it was coming toward us you could hear it It was almost it was intentionally breaking limbs and picking up speed as it was coming
And I asked him, I'm like, do you hear that?
And he's like, yeah.
And I'm like, what do you think that is?
You know, and he didn't know and I didn't know.
And it just, it kept coming.
So needless to say, we finished our business and we got the heck out of there.
I couldn't tell you what it was.
I don't know.
But it did sound bipedal.
And I've never had anything come through the woods like that intentionally.
I say intentionally, making that much noise.
Whatever it was had to be big, and it was intentionally, you know, really breaking limbs.
And, you know, the only thing I can say is maybe it was, maybe it wanted us to leave.
Maybe it was trying to scare us.
I don't know.
But if it didn't, it did.
So we left.
And you know what's crazy, Vic, is we really didn't talk about it after that.
It was just one of those things where we just left.
And, you know, over the years, I kind of forgot about it again until, you know, I started talking about my experience and getting more involved in cryptids and so on.
And, you know, it's one of those things that just kind of came back to me.
So I have no idea what it was, but it got us on the move for sure.
I'm glad you don't know what it was.
If it would have made that perfectly clear, yeah, this would have been a much more intense encounter.
So thank goodness for that.
And it sure sounds to me like it was probably a Sasquatch.
Again, you never saw what it was, so we don't know.
All we can do is guess.
But that sure does sound like Sasquatch behavior to me.
So I bet pretty good money that's what it was.
Well, you know, in that particular area, Vic, there have been, I don't want to say reports, but my middle son, he dates a young lady and her family lived in that area.
And this is years later, you know, and we're talking about this stuff and her family readily admitted that they would see, you know, Sasquatch out in the field behind their house.
So, you know, I don't know.
This was without them knowing anything about my situation.
So who knows, man, you know?
Yeah, anyone's guess is as good as anyone else's.
We're never going to know.
All we can do is guess.
All right, Big D, please tell us about your encounter now.
Give us every last detail that comes to mind.
Well, as we've covered, I did join the Army in 1987.
I spent four years total. Three and a half of those were in Wiesbaden, Germany.
Of course, that was after my basic in AIT, my advanced individual training.
Working as an MP, as I said, you know, I was assigned to the provost marshal's office and it was a great experience.
You know, I think back on it now and, you know, it's kind of tough at the time, but it's never as bad as you as you think it is at the time.
So, at any rate, I've been there a couple of years. I'm thinking this is probably summer of 89 or 90 or so, something like that.
And I was working the graveyard shift as we talked about, and I'm not really sure the time I'm guessing it was probably around 1 am or so something like that. It was on in the morning.
And I was on my way back to the MP station after going out and patrol in the housing area off post. I was on a 4 lane highway.
B-455 to be exact. So anybody that's ever been there and wants to look it up, they can find it.
I was on my way back and I was coming to the underpass, which the underpass is actually
the bridge that goes over B-455 for the access road going back to the East Baden Air Base.
And I know when I say air base, some people say, well, you were in the army. Well, I was.
We had Apache helicopters and Mohawk reconnaissance planes. I think earlier in the
